By the end of the chapter, you should be able to move from a problem statement to a contract, choose iterative or recursive state, justify progress, implement the method in Python, and evaluate correctness and cost.

docs.title: 1. Running problem: count decimal digits
docs.text:
We will design an algorithm for:

Given a non-negative integer, return the number of digits in its decimal representation.

Examples:

0      → 1
7      → 1
42     → 2
1050   → 4

The input 0 immediately reveals an important boundary case. A repeated-division loop naturally performs zero iterations for zero, but the decimal representation still has one digit.

docs.title: 2. Write the contract
docs.text:
Input:
    integer number

Precondition:
    number ≥ 0

Postcondition:
    result equals the number of decimal digits in number

Side effects:
    none

We choose to reject negative input explicitly:

if number < 0:
    raise ValueError("number must be non-negative")

The contract separates semantic requirements from implementation details.

docs.title: Smallest valid input
docs.text: 0 → 1

docs.title: One digit
docs.text: 7 → 1

docs.title: Exact power boundary
docs.text:
10 → 2
100 → 3

docs.title: Internal zero
docs.text: 1050 → 4

docs.title: Invalid input
docs.text:
-8 → ValueError

These cases reveal that counting divisions until zero works for positive values, while zero needs deliberate handling.

docs.title: 4. Choose the state
docs.text:
For positive input, repeatedly removing the final decimal digit by integer division is natural:

number //= 10

State meanings:

remaining = the portion of the original number not yet removed
count = the number of digits already removed

We may modify a local copy so the original parameter meaning remains conceptually available:

remaining = number

docs.title: 5. Design the iterative solution
docs.text:
def digit_count_iterative(number: int) -> int:
    if number < 0:
        raise ValueError("number must be non-negative")

    if number == 0:
        return 1

    remaining = number
    count = 0

    while remaining > 0:
        remaining //= 10
        count += 1

    return count

The special case is part of the specification, not an arbitrary patch.

docs.title: 6. Explain the invariant
docs.text:
For positive input, after each iteration:

count digits have been removed from the right,
and remaining is the original number with those digits removed

A numerical relationship is:

original number = remaining · 10^count + removed suffix

where the removed suffix has fewer than 10^count possible values.

For introductory reasoning, the verbal invariant is sufficient to explain that each iteration accounts for exactly one decimal digit.

docs.title: 7. Explain termination
docs.text:
Use the measure:

remaining

For every positive integer:

0 ≤ remaining // 10 < remaining

Thus the non-negative measure strictly decreases. It eventually becomes zero.

The loop cannot continue forever.

docs.title: 8. Explain completion
docs.text:
When the loop stops:

remaining == 0

No decimal digits remain unprocessed. Since each iteration removed and counted one digit, count is the number of digits in the original positive input.

The special branch establishes the same postcondition for zero.

docs.title: 9. Complexity
docs.text:
For a positive number with d decimal digits, the loop executes d times.

Because:

d = floor(log10(number)) + 1

we may state:

Time: O(d), equivalently O(log number)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

The input model matters. Complexity in terms of numeric magnitude differs from complexity in terms of the number of digits used to represent the input.

docs.title: 10. Recursive design
docs.text:
The same reduction can be recursive.

For positive number:

digits(number) = 1 + digits(number // 10)

A convenient base case is any one-digit non-negative number:

0 ≤ number < 10

Implementation:

def digit_count_recursive(number: int) -> int:
    if number < 0:
        raise ValueError("number must be non-negative")

    if number < 10:
        return 1

    return 1 + digit_count_recursive(number // 10)

The recursive argument strictly decreases by decimal division.

Time is O(d) and call-stack space is O(d).

docs.title: 11. Compare the two designs
docs.text:
Both versions use the same conceptual reduction: remove one decimal digit.

The iterative version stores progress in:

remaining, count

The recursive version stores pending additions in the call stack.

For this linear reduction, the iterative version is usually operationally preferable in Python because it uses constant auxiliary space. The recursive version makes the recurrence especially visible.

docs.title: Step 1 — State the problem precisely
docs.text: Identify valid inputs and required outputs.

docs.title: Step 2 — Write the contract
docs.text: Include preconditions, postconditions, side effects, and failure behaviour.

docs.title: Step 3 — Build examples
docs.text: Include ordinary, smallest, repeated, extreme, and invalid cases.

docs.title: Step 4 — Choose a representation
docs.text: Decide which primitive or compound values will represent the state.

docs.title: Step 5 — Choose iteration or recursion
docs.text: Match the control structure to the problem shape.

docs.title: Step 6 — State the meaning of state
docs.text: For loops, define accumulators, counters, indices, and processed regions. For recursion, define what each call returns for its subproblem.

docs.title: Step 7 — Establish the base or initial case
docs.text: The state meaning must be true before repetition.

docs.title: Step 8 — Define progress
docs.text: A loop update or recursive argument must move toward completion.

docs.title: Step 9 — Justify correctness
docs.text: Use invariant preservation or correctness of smaller instances.

docs.title: Step 10 — Justify termination
docs.text: Use a bounded decreasing measure.

docs.title: Step 11 — Analyse complexity
docs.text: Count iterations, calls, operations, and auxiliary storage.

docs.title: Step 12 — Test against the specification
docs.text: Use examples to expose defects and validate the implementation.

docs.title: Wrong domain
docs.text: If negative integers are accepted unintentionally, integer division behaviour may not approach zero as expected.

docs.title: Wrong base case
docs.text: A recursive base case number == 0 with 1 + recurse(number // 10) gives the wrong count structure unless handled carefully.

docs.title: Wrong update order
docs.text: Counting before deciding whether a valid digit remains can create off-by-one errors.

docs.title: Hidden mutation
docs.text: Destroying the only copy of the original input may prevent later validation or reporting.

docs.title: Vague termination
docs.text: “The number gets smaller” should be replaced by a precise strict inequality in a bounded domain.

docs.title: Complexity stated in the wrong variable
docs.text: O(number) is not the cost of repeated division by ten. The number of iterations is logarithmic in the magnitude.

docs.title: 14. Correctness and testing cooperate
docs.text:
Reasoning covers the general class of valid inputs. Tests target likely weaknesses.

def _test_digit_count() -> None:
    assert digit_count_iterative(0) == 1
    assert digit_count_iterative(7) == 1
    assert digit_count_iterative(10) == 2
    assert digit_count_iterative(1050) == 4

    try:
        digit_count_iterative(-1)
    except ValueError:
        pass
    else:
        raise AssertionError("negative input should be rejected")

Testing does not prove the invariant or termination. It checks whether the implementation agrees with selected consequences of the specification.

docs.title: 15. What you must be able to explain
docs.text:
After this chapter, you should be able to:

  • derive an algorithm from a contract;
  • use examples before implementation;
  • choose iterative and recursive state;
  • justify a special boundary case;
  • state an invariant or recursive meaning;
  • identify a termination measure;
  • compare time and auxiliary space;
  • explain the role of tests without confusing them with proof.

docs.title: Exercise 1 — Sum of digits
docs.text: Design iterative and recursive functions returning the sum of decimal digits of a non-negative integer.

docs.title: Exercise 2 — Count occurrences of a digit
docs.text: Given number and digit, count how many decimal positions equal digit. Handle number == 0 carefully.

docs.title: Exercise 3 — Integer exponentiation
docs.text: Construct iterative and recursive algorithms for a non-negative exponent. Then improve the recursive version using exponentiation by squaring.

docs.title: Exercise 4 — Palindrome sequence
docs.text: Design an iterative algorithm that checks whether a list reads the same forward and backward. State the invariant and shrinking-interval measure.

docs.title: Exercise 5 — First divisor
docs.text: Return the smallest divisor greater than one of an integer n > 1. State the stopping boundary and complexity.

docs.title: 17. Summary
docs.text:
A correct algorithm is built by connecting specification, state, progress, and completion. Iteration and recursion are not isolated syntax patterns; they are control models whose correctness must be derived from explicit meanings and termination arguments.

The next chapter applies the module's ideas in several complete worked examples.

docs.title: Why worked examples matter
docs.text:
A definition becomes useful only when it can guide a complete solution. This chapter combines the module's ideas in several problems. Each example follows the same sequence:

  1. state the problem;
  2. write the contract;
  3. choose state or recursive meaning;
  4. implement in Python;
  5. explain correctness;
  6. explain termination;
  7. analyse complexity;
  8. identify boundary cases.

The purpose is not to memorise the functions. The purpose is to recognise a reusable design process.

docs.title: Example 1 — Count values in a closed interval
Problem
docs.text: Given a list of integers and two bounds lower and upper, count how many values satisfy lower ≤ value ≤ upper.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Precondition:
    lower ≤ upper

Postcondition:
    result equals the number of elements value in values
    satisfying lower ≤ value ≤ upper

Side effects:
    values is not modified

docs.title: State meaning
docs.text: count = number of matching elements in the processed prefix

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def count_in_range(values: list[int], lower: int, upper: int) -> int:
    if lower > upper:
        raise ValueError("lower must not exceed upper")

    count = 0

    for value in values:
        if lower <= value <= upper:
            count += 1

    return count

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text: Initially the processed prefix is empty and count == 0. Each new value either satisfies the predicate and increases the count by one, or does not and leaves the count unchanged. At completion, the processed prefix is the whole list.

docs.title: Termination and complexity
docs.text:
The finite list is traversed once.

Time: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

docs.title: Boundary cases
docs.text:
• empty list → 0;
  • lower == upper → counts occurrences of one value;
  • invalid reversed bounds → ValueError.

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Return the first index i such that values[i] < values[i - 1], or -1 if the list is non-decreasing.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Postcondition:
    result = -1 if every adjacent pair is non-decreasing;
    otherwise result is the smallest index i > 0 with values[i] < values[i - 1]

docs.title: State meaning
docs.text:
Before inspecting index i:

values[0:i] is non-decreasing

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def first_order_violation(values: list[int]) -> int:
    for index in range(1, len(values)):
        if values[index] < values[index - 1]:
            return index

    return -1

docs.title: Correctness
docs.text: The loop checks adjacent pairs from left to right. If a violation is returned, every earlier pair has already been verified, so the returned index is the first violation. If no return occurs, every adjacent pair satisfies the required order.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
Best case: O(1)
Worst case: O(n)
Auxiliary space: O(1)

docs.title: Example 3 — Integer logarithm by repeated division
Problem
docs.text: For integers number ≥ 1 and base ≥ 2, return the largest integer k such that base^k ≤ number.

docs.title: Idea
docs.text: Repeatedly divide by base and count how many divisions are possible before the quotient becomes smaller than base.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def integer_log(number: int, base: int) -> int:
    if number < 1:
        raise ValueError("number must be at least 1")
    if base < 2:
        raise ValueError("base must be at least 2")

    exponent = 0
    remaining = number

    while remaining >= base:
        remaining //= base
        exponent += 1

    return exponent

docs.title: Invariant
docs.text:
number = remaining · base^exponent + discarded remainder information,
and exponent counts completed divisions

A simpler interpretation is that after exponent divisions, remaining equals the integer quotient after removing exponent base factors.

docs.title: Termination
docs.text:
For remaining ≥ base ≥ 2:

remaining // base < remaining

The positive measure strictly decreases.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
O(log_base number) time
O(1) space

docs.title: Problem
docs.text: Compute the greatest common divisor of two non-negative integers, not both zero.

docs.title: Contract
docs.text:
Precondition:
    first ≥ 0
    second ≥ 0
    first > 0 or second > 0

Postcondition:
    result is the greatest positive integer dividing both original inputs

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def gcd_iterative(first: int, second: int) -> int:
    if first < 0 or second < 0:
        raise ValueError("arguments must be non-negative")
    if first == 0 and second == 0:
        raise ValueError("at least one argument must be positive")

    while second != 0:
        first, second = second, first % second

    return first

docs.title: Invariant
docs.text:
gcd(first, second) equals the gcd of the original inputs

The transformation preserves the set of common divisors.

docs.title: Termination
docs.text: The second value becomes a remainder strictly smaller than the previous positive second value.

docs.title: Example 5 — Recursive Euclid algorithm
docs.text:
def gcd_recursive(first: int, second: int) -> int:
    if first < 0 or second < 0:
        raise ValueError("arguments must be non-negative")
    if first == 0 and second == 0:
        raise ValueError("at least one argument must be positive")

    if second == 0:
        return first

    return gcd_recursive(second, first % second)

The recursive meaning is direct:

gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b)

Time complexity is logarithmic under the standard analysis. Stack depth is also logarithmic in the magnitude of the arguments.

docs.title: Iterative version
docs.text:
def power_iterative(base: int, exponent: int) -> int:
    if exponent < 0:
        raise ValueError("exponent must be non-negative")

    result = 1

    for _ in range(exponent):
        result *= base

    return result

Invariant:

result = base^k after k completed iterations

Complexity:

O(exponent) time
O(1) space

docs.title: Recursive version
docs.text:
def power_recursive(base: int, exponent: int) -> int:
    if exponent < 0:
        raise ValueError("exponent must be non-negative")

    if exponent == 0:
        return 1

    return base * power_recursive(base, exponent - 1)

Complexity:

O(exponent) time
O(exponent) stack space

docs.title: Example 7 — Exponentiation by squaring
docs.text:
A better recursive reduction uses parity.

def power_fast(base: int, exponent: int) -> int:
    if exponent < 0:
        raise ValueError("exponent must be non-negative")

    if exponent == 0:
        return 1

    half = power_fast(base, exponent // 2)

    if exponent % 2 == 0:
        return half * half

    return base * half * half

The exponent is approximately halved at each call.

Time: O(log exponent)
Stack space: O(log exponent)

This example shows that recursive design is not only a stylistic alternative. A better reduction can improve asymptotic complexity.

docs.title: Example 8 — Palindrome check with two indices
Problem
docs.text: Return True if a list reads the same forward and backward.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
def is_palindrome(values: list[int]) -> bool:
    left = 0
    right = len(values) - 1

    while left < right:
        if values[left] != values[right]:
            return False

        left += 1
        right -= 1

    return True

docs.title: Invariant
docs.text:
All positions outside the interval [left, right]
have already been matched with their mirror positions.

docs.title: Termination measure
docs.text:
right - left + 1

The active interval shrinks by two per successful iteration.

docs.title: Complexity
docs.text:
O(n) time
O(1) auxiliary space

docs.title: Example 9 — Recursive palindrome check
docs.text:
def is_palindrome_recursive(
    values: list[int],
    left: int = 0,
    right: int | None = None,
) -> bool:
    if right is None:
        right = len(values) - 1

    if left >= right:
        return True

    if values[left] != values[right]:
        return False

    return is_palindrome_recursive(values, left + 1, right - 1)

The recursive subproblem is the interior interval.

The interval length strictly decreases. Time is O(n) and stack space is O(n).

docs.title: Example 10 — Classify values into a record-like result
docs.text:
Compound output can preserve several related statistics.

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class Classification:
    negative: int
    zero: int
    positive: int


def classify(values: list[int]) -> Classification:
    negative = 0
    zero = 0
    positive = 0

    for value in values:
        if value < 0:
            negative += 1
        elif value == 0:
            zero += 1
        else:
            positive += 1

    return Classification(negative, zero, positive)

Invariant:

negative + zero + positive = number of processed elements,
and each counter records its corresponding class

This example connects iteration, selection, primitive counters, and compound output.

docs.title: What the examples have in common
docs.text:
Although the problems differ, each solution answers the same structural questions:

  • What is the input domain?
  • What result is required?
  • Which state summarises progress?
  • What is initially true?
  • How is one smaller or later state produced?
  • Why is the meaning preserved?
  • Why must the process stop?
  • What is the cost?

That repeated structure is the real content of the module.

docs.title: Treści kształcenia
docs.text: L.p. Treść kształcenia (tematyka zajęć) Wykład — studia stacjonarne Wykład — studia niestacjonarne Ćwiczenia — studia stacjonarne Ćwiczenia — studia niestacjonarne 1 Przetwarzanie imperatywne. Asercja początkowa i końcowa algorytmu. Postacie algorytmu. Cechy algorytmów. Pseudokod. Podstawowe metody strukturalizacji algorytmów. 1 1 2 1 2 Pętle iteracyjne, warunek stopu pętli iteracyjnej. Rekurencja. Schemat konstruowania poprawnego algorytmu. Typy pierwotne i złożone. 1 1 4 1 3 Tablica i rekord jako agregaty danych. Reprezentacja danych różnych typów w pamięci. Bezpośredni dostęp do składowych. Podstawowe operacje na tablicach. 2 1 3 1 4 Wyszukiwanie liniowe i binarne. Metody sortowania. Cechy algorytmów sortowania. Złożoność obliczeniowa algorytmów. Metoda „dziel i zwyciężaj”. Schemat algorytmu Quick-Sort. Tablica indeksowa. Algorytmy rekurencyjne. 2 2 4 1 5 Komunikacja funkcji z otoczeniem, efekty uboczne. Zjawiska na stosie dla zmiennych. Funkcje rekurencyjne. Anatomia wywołania rekurencyjnego. Rekurencja pośrednia i zagnieżdżona. Implementacja stosowa rekurencji. Kłopoty z rekurencją. Rekurencja a iteracja. 5 2 8 4 6 Algorytmy z powrotami. Metody usprawniania algorytmów zachłannych: systematyczne, heurystyczne. Dynamiczne struktury danych. 1 1 4 2 7 Listy liniowe. LIFO-stosy i FIFO-kolejki, kolejki z priorytetem, listy dwukierunkowe i cykliczne. Samoorganizujące się listy. Listy z przeskokami. Niektóre algorytmy obsługi list. 2 2 5 2 8 Drzewa binarne. Podstawowe pojęcia dotyczące drzew. Drzewa binarne. Przechodzenie po drzewie binarnym. Drzewo binarnych poszukiwań. Wykorzystanie drzew do budowy słownika. Równoważenie drzew. Drzewo AVL. Sterty. 5 2 6 2 9 Grafy. Metody reprezentacji grafu. Analiza własności przykładowych grafów. Podstawowe operacje na grafach. Implementacja grafu w postaci listy incydencji. Algorytm szukania w głąb dla grafu jako przykład wyszukiwania wyczerpującego i jego zastosowania. Przykłady wykorzystania algorytmu szukania w głąb dla grafu. 6 2 7 1 10 Analiza złożoności czasowej algorytmów. Złożoność obliczeniowa. O-notacja. Przykłady obliczeń złożoności. Problemy algorytmicznie trudne. Problemy N- i NP-zupełne. 6 2 2 1 Razem 30 16 45 16

docs.text: Sposób zaliczenia Laboratoria Wykład Egzamin ustny Egzamin pisemny 90% Kolokwium 80% Przygotowanie i przedstawienie eseju / referatu / innej formy wypowiedzi pisemnej Wykonanie zadań / ćwiczeń / projektu podczas zajęć 20% Wykonanie zadań / ćwiczeń / projektu poza zajęciami Wypowiedzi ustne podczas zajęć (np. w trakcie dyskusji, debaty) 10% Inny obecność na zajęciach Razem 100% 100%

docs.title: Rozliczenie pracy własnej studenta
docs.text: L.p. Czynności w ramach pracy własnej Szacowana liczba godzin — studia stacjonarne Szacowana liczba godzin — studia niestacjonarne 1 Przygotowanie do udziału w zajęciach (np. wstępna lektura, przygotowanie lub zgromadzenie materiałów, pomocy, przygotowanie referatu lub prezentacji na zajęcia itp.) 2 Wykonanie ćwiczeń lub zadań po zajęciach (jako utrwalenie lub rozszerzenie treści z odbytych zajęć) 15 25 3 Realizacja zadania projektowego poza zajęciami 4 Przygotowanie pracy pisemnej poza zajęciami 5 Lektura obowiązkowa 6 Obowiązkowe zapoznanie się z innymi materiałami lub treściami (np. materiałami audio, wideo, narzędziami, pomocami, oprogramowaniem, sprzętem, aktami prawnymi, dokumentacją, warunkami miejsca pracy itp.) 6 Przygotowanie do kolokwium 25 40 7 Przygotowanie do egzaminu 10 20 8 Inne … Razem 50 85
